It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11633647]] Wikidata name: Toyokawa Inari Betsuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in [[:en:Moto-Akasaka|Moto-Akasaka]], Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji]] in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]].
== History == The temple traces its origins to [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]

It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>

== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].

The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the [[:en:Sixteen Arhats|Sixteen Arhats]] and [[:en:Rāgarāja|Rāgarāja]]. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.toyokawainari-tokyo.jp/ Official Website]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11633647]] Wikidata name: Toyokawa Inari Betsuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in [[:en:Moto-Akasaka|Moto-Akasaka]], Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji]] in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]].
== History == The temple traces its origins to [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the [[:en:Sixteen Arhats|Sixteen Arhats]] and [[:en:Rāgarāja|Rāgarāja]]. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].

[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in [[:en:Moto-Akasaka|Moto-Akasaka]], Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji]] in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]].
== History == The temple traces its origins to [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the [[:en:Sixteen Arhats|Sixteen Arhats]] and [[:en:Rāgarāja|Rāgarāja]]. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.toyokawainari-tokyo.jp/ Official Website]

{{Inari Faith}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo}} [[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11633647}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11633647]] Wikidata name: Toyokawa Inari Betsuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in [[:en:Moto-Akasaka|Moto-Akasaka]], Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji]] in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]].
== History == The temple traces its origins to [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the [[:en:Sixteen Arhats|Sixteen Arhats]] and [[:en:Rāgarāja|Rāgarāja]]. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.toyokawainari-tokyo.jp/ Official Website]

== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].

{{wikidata link|Q11633647}} [[Category:Buddhist temples in Tokyo]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Inari temples]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Soto temples]] [[Category:Temples in Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11633647]] Wikidata name: Toyokawa Inari Betsuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in [[:en:Moto-Akasaka|Moto-Akasaka]], Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji]] in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]].
== History == The temple traces its origins to [[:en:Ōoka Tadasuke|Ōoka Tadasuke]] (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the [[:en:Sixteen Arhats|Sixteen Arhats]] and [[:en:Rāgarāja|Rāgarāja]]. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].

== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the [[:en:Sixteen Arhats|Sixteen Arhats]] and [[:en:Rāgarāja|Rāgarāja]]. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line]] and [[:en:Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line]].

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11633647]] Wikidata name: Toyokawa Inari Betsuin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke}} | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|en|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557|lt=Moto-Akasaka}}, Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji}} in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}.
== History == The temple traces its origins to {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke}} (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}} to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|WD=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats}} and {{ill|Rāgarāja|en|Rāgarāja|WD=Q1188900|lt=Rāgarāja}}. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|en|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|WD=Q1073046|lt=Tokyo Metro Ginza Line}} and {{ill|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|en|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|WD=Q1147028|lt=Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.toyokawainari-tokyo.jp/ Official Website]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo]][[ja:豊川稲荷東京別院]][[pl:Toyokawa Inari]]
{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke}} | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|en|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557|lt=Moto-Akasaka}}, Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji}} in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}.
== History == The temple traces its origins to {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke}} (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}} to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|WD=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats}} and {{ill|Rāgarāja|en|Rāgarāja|WD=Q1188900|lt=Rāgarāja}}. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|en|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|WD=Q1073046|lt=Tokyo Metro Ginza Line}} and {{ill|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|en|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|WD=Q1147028|lt=Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.toyokawainari-tokyo.jp/ Official Website]

== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|WD=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats|1=Sixteen Arhats}} and {{ill|Rāgarāja|en|Rāgarāja|WD=Q1188900|lt=Rāgarāja|1=Rāgarāja}}. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|en|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|WD=Q1073046|lt=Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|1=Ginza Line}} and {{ill|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|en|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|WD=Q1147028|lt=Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|1=Marunouchi Line}}.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085|1=Sōtō}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224|1=Dakini}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke|1=Ōoka Tadasuke}} | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557|1=Moto-Akasaka}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|1=Ōoka Tadasuke}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|en|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557|lt=Moto-Akasaka|1=Moto-Akasaka}}, Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji|1=Toyokawa Inari}} in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}.
== History == The temple traces its origins to {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke|1=Ōoka Tadasuke}} (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}} to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|WD=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats|1=Sixteen Arhats}} and {{ill|Rāgarāja|en|Rāgarāja|WD=Q1188900|lt=Rāgarāja|1=Rāgarāja}}. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|en|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|WD=Q1073046|lt=Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|1=Ginza Line}} and {{ill|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|en|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|WD=Q1147028|lt=Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|1=Marunouchi Line}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.toyokawainari-tokyo.jp/ Official Website]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Sōtō|WD=Q749085|1=Sōtō}} |image = File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150036.jpg | Caption = main hall | location = 1-4-7 Moto-Akasaka, Minato, Tokyo, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35.676677777777776|N|139.73300555555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Dakini|WD=Q1127224|1=Dakini}} | founded = [[Bunsei]] 11 (1828) | Founder = {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke|1=Ōoka Tadasuke}} | Official name = Myōgon-ji Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin | Cultural Properties = | map = Tokyo city | prefecture= {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557|1=Moto-Akasaka}} | founded_by= {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|1=Ōoka Tadasuke}} }}
[[File:Oka-tadasuke-byo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Ōoka Mausoleum (housing the ancestral tablet of Tadasuke Ōoka, transferred from his family temple Jōken-ji in Chigasaki)]]Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin** (豊川稲荷東京別院) is a Buddhist temple of the [[:en:Sōtō Zen|Sōtō Zen]] sect located in {{ill|Moto-Akasaka|en|Moto-Akasaka|WD=Q864557|lt=Moto-Akasaka|1=Moto-Akasaka}}, Minato, Tokyo. It is the only directly governed branch temple (enclave) of {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari Myōgon-ji|1=Toyokawa Inari}} in [[:en:Toyokawa|Toyokawa]], {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}.
== History == The temple traces its origins to {{ill|Ōoka Tadasuke|en|Ōoka Tadasuke|WD=Q1054143|lt=Ōoka Tadasuke|1=Ōoka Tadasuke}} (commonly known as Ōoka Echizen-no-Kami), who enshrined the deity [[Dakini|Dakini-ten]] from Toyokawa Inari at his Edo residence as a private Inari shrine. The Ōoka family had been devout followers of Toyokawa Inari since their time in Mikawa Province. The shrine was later moved to the Ōoka residence in Akasaka.
=== Establishment === During the Edo period, Inari worship was widespread, and the Ōoka family opened their estate shrine to the public on "Days of the Horse" and the 22nd day of each month. In 1828, in response to requests from devotees, Myōgon-ji borrowed a portion of the Ōoka estate and established a dedicated site for Edo worshippers, marking the foundation of the Tokyo Betsuin.
In 1876, due to government restrictions on public worship at private shrines, the deity's spirit was temporarily returned to Toyokawa Myōgon-ji. Two years later, the ban was lifted, and public worship resumed. In 1887, the temple moved to its current location in Moto-Akasaka due to space limitations at the original site.
=== Devotion === Ōoka Tadasuke is celebrated for his rise from a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}} to a [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] and his service as a [[town magistrate]] in Edo. Worship at Toyokawa Inari Tokyo Betsuin became associated with success, protection from theft, and recovery of lost items. After the Meiji era, Akasaka's development as a cultural hub attracted further patronage, including from entertainers, sports figures, and celebrities.
== annual Events == - **Major festivals**: Held in January, May, and September on the 22nd. ** Continuous prayer services, with outdoor tea ceremonies in fair weather. - **Traffic Safety Prayer festival**: Held on June 22, featuring monks collecting donations for traffic accident orphans. - **Okōmori-kai**: An overnight ritual held in November for devotees unable to travel to Toyokawa.
== Grounds == [[File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150051.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Sanmon Gate]] [[File:Kano-inari-sonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|thumb|220px|right|Kanō Inari]]
The temple grounds offer a serene atmosphere away from the bustle of the city.
- **main hall**: Houses the temple’s primary deity, Dakini Shinten, alongside statues of the {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|WD=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats|1=Sixteen Arhats}} and {{ill|Rāgarāja|en|Rāgarāja|WD=Q1188900|lt=Rāgarāja|1=Rāgarāja}}. It also features a distribution center for amulets and talismans. - **Sub-Shrines**: ** *Kanō Inari*: Specializes in severing bad ties. ** *Yūzū Inari*: Offers ten-yen coins to borrow and return with interest after fulfilling a wish. ** *Zeniarai Benzaiten*: Associated with multiplying washed coins. ** *Daikokudō*: Dedicated to Daikokuten, recently reconstructed.
- **Hōrinkaku**: Located in the Inari Hall on the second floor, where [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] Buddha is enshrined. It serves as the main prayer hall and hosts events such as sutra copying and lectures. - **Tea Shops and Cultural Center**: The grounds feature tea shops selling traditional offerings and snacks. The second floor of the cultural center is used for classes and workshops.
<gallery widths="180px" heights="150px"> File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150038.jpg|Main grounds (in front of the three deity shrines) File:Toyokawa-inari Tokyo 0612150045.jpg|Spirit Fox Shrine File:Benzaisonten (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Zeniarai Benzaiten File:Daikokudo (Toyokawa-inari, Tokyo).jpg|Daikokudō </gallery>
== Other Toyokawa Inari Shrines == - **Ōoka Inari Shrine** (Okazaki City, Aichi Prefecture): established at the former residence of the Ōoka family. - **Toyokawa Inari Shrine** (Bunkyō, Tokyo): Linked to another branch of the Ōoka family.
== Access == - A 5-minute walk from [[:en:Akasaka-Mitsuke Station|Akasaka-Mitsuke Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|en|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|WD=Q1073046|lt=Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|1=Ginza Line}} and {{ill|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|en|Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|WD=Q1147028|lt=Tokyo Metro Marunouchi Line|1=Marunouchi Line}}.
